title: Theory of melting lines with a variable enthalpy of fusion
abstract: Conventional derivations of phase boundaries from the Clausius-Clapeyron (CC) relation often employ the constant latent heat approximation to maintain analytical functions of the sublimation and boiling curves. To address the complex thermodynamics of the solid-liquid transition, we develop a two-phase analytical model by modifying the CC equation to account for a variable enthalpy of fusion along the melting line (ML). Our methodology utilizes recent theoretical and experimental progress demonstrating that the isobaric heat capacity of crystalline solids near the melting point features a dominant anharmonic, volume-dependent component. Consequently, the latent heat is correlated to the specific volumes of the coexisting phases. Differentiation of this modified CC relation yields a second-order differential equation governing ML. By imposing appropriate e boundary conditions, physically acceptable approximate parabolic solutions are derived. The parameters of these analytic functions are defined exclusively by fundamental thermophysical properties, including the bulk moduli, thermal expansion coefficients, and specific volumes of the coexisting phases, as well as the isobaric heat capacity of the solid. Our derivation, rooted in solid-state anharmonicity, yields approximate parabolic scaling laws that corroborate with a recent universal model derived from the Phonon Theory of Liquids [K. Trachenko, Phys. Rev. E 109, 034122 (2024)], supporting the universal parabolic nature of melting curves from a completely distinct theoretical foundation.
summary: This paper proposes a new theoretical framework for calculating melting lines by accounting for the variable enthalpy of fusion caused by solid-state anharmonicity. The resulting model predicts a universal parabolic trajectory for phase boundaries that is consistent with both recent phonon-based theories and experimental observations across diverse materials.
main_problem: The difficulty in defining a universal analytical equation for melting lines in P-T phase diagrams due to the inadequacy of the constant latent heat approximation in the Clausius-Clapeyron relation.
main_result: The derivation of a universal parabolic scaling law for melting lines, which aligns with the Phonon Theory of Liquids and matches experimental data for various substances.
method: A two-phase analytical model that modifies the Clausius-Clapeyron equation to include a variable enthalpy of fusion correlated to the specific volumes of coexisting phases.
model_or_system: A thermodynamic model of the solid-liquid phase transition (melting) applicable to a wide range of condensed matter, including noble gases, molecular systems, metals, and semiconductors.
key_observables: Melting lines (P-T phase boundaries), enthalpy of fusion, and the slope of the melting curve.
important_parameters: Enthalpy of fusion (delta H), scaling parameter epsilon (Cp/beta), bulk moduli, thermal expansion coefficients, and specific volumes.
main_assumptions: The dominance of the anharmonic, volume-dependent component of heat capacity near the melting point and the assumption that the scaling parameter epsilon remains constant over narrow pressure ranges.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of melting line derivation, develops a modified Clausius-Clapeyron relation based on variable enthalpy, derives a second-order differential equation for the melting line, provides analytical parabolic solutions, and validates the results against existing theories and experimental data.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
